sentences.text: My dad had the idea that if we had some seeds, we could grow some food.
But prior to that-- once again, I got ahead of myself-- before we moved to this particular little house, my mother couldn't accept what was happening around her.
She just blocked everything out.
And at one point, when we still could get some food, if we went and stood in line for four hours, five hours, to get a piece of meat, when we brought it, my mother would get mad at us.
What did you bring me?
What kind of meat is that?
I can't cook something like that.
Because she didn't want to realize what was going on.
She didn't want to accept it.
So she was ailing.
And by the time we moved to the little house, she was quite sick.
She was suffering from malnutrition.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And there was nothing we could do for her.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
One day while I was outside, I-- everything was happening so fast.
I saw people running, running into the houses.
And by the time I realized what was happening, I saw this man on a horse-- this German on a horse.
And I heard stories that there was a German who rides through the streets of the ghetto on a horse, and whoever is in his way, he'll trample him to death, or beat him to death.
So I-- I just sat down on this-- on the sidewalk, and I went like this, you know?
I got-- I got scared.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
And this German jumped on the horse, and he looks at me and he says, my god-- is this [?
Manusha? ?] And I look up, and it was [?
Voik-- ?] the [? friends ?] who went to beat up the Jews in the barrack.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] seeing who it was, I just didn't pay any attention to-- to what I heard, because it couldn't be [?
Voik. ?]
So he says, where is your father?
Where is your mother?
Where do you live?
And he picked me up, put me and his shoulders, and he says, direct me to your home.
So it was about, like, half a block down the street where we lived.
So I brought him home, and he walked in, and he saw my mother in bed.
So first thing he did, he was-- he grabbed her, he was holding her and dancing with her.
And he started yelling at my father.
How come you didn't come to see me?
How could you let her get so sick?
And my father says, what are you talking about?
How could I come to see you?
What are you doing here?
And he proceeded to tell my father that he's now the head of the criminal police in the ghetto.
Now, we heard about the criminal police-- we all knew about it, that once someone was taken to the criminal police, you never heard or saw him anymore.
You did hear screams coming out of there.
We knew that terrible things were going on there.
So [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] immediately, he told my father, you come and see me, and I'll take care of you.
So he spent some times with us, telling stories about his wife, who was my sister's girlfriend, and about the baby that he has.
And at the same time, he wanted to know all about us.
And then he left.
And my father promised him that he'll come and see him the next day.
And my father went.
That was the first and last time my father went.
He came back.
He told my mother, and he told us, that [?
Voik ?] propositioned him that since my father knew so many people, and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] he would give him everything and take care of us if my father cooperated with him.
And my father told him, you-- I'm surprised at you, because you know me, and that you even suggest such a thing.
He said, I'd rather starve.
And that was the last time my father ever saw [?
Voik. ?] But my sister went one morning to see him, and she told him how sick my mother is.
And she said, I don't want nothing from you, but tell your wife.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Her being such a good friend of mine, maybe she'll help me.
So he told my sister, OK, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I'll tell my wife.
He says, you come back tomorrow morning.
And my sister used to stand in the doorway.
She used to tell us-- she used to hide.
She didn't want to anyone to see her talking to him.
Because if you just talk to a German in the criminal police, people would right away point their fingers at you and say you're a cooperator-- collaborator.
So-- because people don't know the relationship we had with this man before the war.
So, the next morning-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I think my sister told him what was wrong with my mother, because the next morning, he brought a box of vitamin injections.
And he said, you give this to your mother.
You have a doctor give it to your mother, and she'll get stronger.
And you come in the afternoon, and I will tell the people working here to give you food.
So my sister went in the afternoon, and she got food.
But there again is a very interesting story, because the man who was working there, who was a collaborator, was a cousin of my father's.
The man had the same name as we had.
So he was not nice to her, because he was afraid.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So he made her wait before he gave anything, and he carried on like he was the boss there, you know?
But the few times that she went there, he had to give her food.
Then she-- when she brought back the medication for my mother, my father found a doctor who was an old man from-- they were sent i into the ghetto from Czechoslovakia.
And he was a professor before the war.
So my father spoke to him, and he said, OK, he's going to give my mother the injections.
So he used to come and give her, every day, an injection.
And she started to show improvement.
She could already get off the bed, and start a little walking.
But she wasn't still well, really.
And one day, my brother said to my dad, you know, Dad, I don't think that this doctor is a nice man.
And my dad said, well, what do you mean?
He says, he's not giving Mama the injections.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I forgot to tell you that, after my mother started feeling well, then she slipped back.
And then my-- then my brother said, he's not giving my mother injections.
And my dad said, what do you mean he's not giving?
He says, well, I saw him hide it-- the vial-- and I saw him take water from the tap.
And he's giving her only water.
And my father said, I can't believe that a man like that would do something like that.
So my father watched him through the window.
Because when he used to give my mother the injection, he told everybody to leave the room.
And my dad watched him through the window, and sure enough--
So my dad walked in and asked him, how come he's doing it?
And he started to sob, to cry.
And he said his wife is sick, and he wants to help her.
So [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] you know, how could you fault a man for admitting, in-- in circumstances like that?
My dad said, OK, fine.
I won't need you anymore.
Because he wouldn't trust him anymore.
So my mother got well after that.
She was well enough to, you know, to function.
They continued for quite awhile.
Then-- by then, we started to get a little food rations.
You see, the ghetto was growing in experiments, and so even though the rations were very small, but we were getting something.
And one of the things my sister got from [?
Voik ?] was seeds.
And my father and my brother was working on the garden.
I don't think I told you that my two oldest brothers were not in the ghetto, because they left.
They-- when things started to get very bad, they registered to go to work in Germany, hoping that over there, they're going to get a little food.
So they left in the beginning of 1941.
So it was just my father, my mother, my sister, my brother, and myself.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And by then, like I said, we started to get a little rations.
We started-- if you worked, you got a soup.
So everybody went to work, including my mother.
Whatever jobs you could get.
My mother was very handy with knitting, so she got a job in a knitting factory.
My sister got a job, also, for a while, in a knitting factory.
Excuse me.
My brother was working for a company that was-- a letter company.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I went to work in a-- what we called a [NON-ENGLISH] factory.
We used to make shoes out of old rags.
We used to make slippers and boots-- warm boots for the German soldiers.
I worked there for a while.
And once you worked, you got a soup.
So that was like-- you know, you could function.
You had something to eat.
The soup was mostly water, but-- [LAUGHS]
No.
No money.
We did not get paid.
The only monies we got was from Germany from my two brothers.
We got seven--
Through the ghetto.
Through the post office in the ghetto.
At that time, you still could-- we still had mail, because we used to-- I remember getting letters from them, and sending letters to them.
And we used to get $7 a month per worker.
So we had two brothers, so we used to get 14 marks a month.
And it wasn't such a good thing, because a lot of people got killed very early because of that.
[LAUGHS] You're surprised?
Because when-- the Germans were very smart, because they figured, if they kill the ones, the family who gets the money, they won't have to pay them.
So they were the first ones to be taken out from the ghetto.
And it was a known fact that any family that had someone working in Germany would soon disappear.
So [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] we had-- like I said, we had already planted a garden.
We had a little food.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I don't remember where or how we got fuel-- coal, because that's what we used, or wood.
I really don't remember.
But I remember it was much better-- it wasn't as cold as it was when we had nothing.
So this was the winter of '42?
And we are working already.
The ghetto is completely locked up.
There is no way in or out, unless you work for the Germans, with the Germans, whatever.
At this point, we started hearing stories that-- see, the ghetto was divided by a bridge.
There were two sides to the ghetto.
Where we lived was, before the war, known to be the Polish section.
Where the ghetto itself, the big part of the ghetto, was where the Jewish people used to live before the war.
And they had to build a bridge because the street itself had a line-- a trolley car line.
And the Polacks and the Germans had to ride the trolley car.
So they made a fence of each-- on each side of the street, and then they built a bridge.
So the factories were mostly on the other side, too.
So every day we had to walk to work, we had to go through the bridge.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I really don't remember where we got the fuel from, but it wasn't that cold anymore.
I never remember having it that cold after that.
And my dad, in turn, decided that, since we were all going to be working, he felt that someone should keep the family together.
And the only way to keep the families together, if there's someone going to be in charge of the family.
So my dad decided that he's going to be a janitor.
And by being a janitor in the building [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] next to our little house, he'll be home all the time.
He'll do his work, and he'll be home.
So when he's home, he can do some laundry or he can do some cooking-- because we used to get a ration of food, by then.
We used to get a couple of potatoes, or everybody got a piece of bread that we used to take it as one bread.
And this way, have a little warm tea or something when you come home from work.
And this worked out pretty good, because we never came to an empty house.
We always came to a house that was a home-- not like everybody that I knew came into a cold, empty house.
So-- and also, it gave him an opportunity, while he was home alone, to invite a lot of people who did not work for one reason or another.
And there were always people-- especially young people.

All my friends, my sister's friends, my brother's friends knew that this was at home that you can come into and get away from your problems, because my father used to put on shows, used to teach them songs and dancing.
And he always used to say, this will pass.
You have to have hope.
This will pass.
And we have to study, and we have to learn, and we have to be happy.
And we have to-- yeah.

And it was-- our home became like a gathering place for all these young people who had, by then, either lost parents, or had parents who were sick, because almost everybody had someone very sick from malnutrition.
So-- and my father kept the family together like that, and kept the young people together.
And then-- let's see.
Things started to happen.
We heard that on the other side of the ghetto, the Germans had what they called actions, that they were taking people off the streets.
We heard all kind of stories.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] my father used to say, no, it couldn't be.
Why would they grab somebody off the street?
Why wouldn't they say, if you want to work, register?
You know, he still didn't believe it.
And then one day, there was a terrible, terrible edict that they gave out, and that was that all children under I think it was 14-- I'm not sure the age-- have to be delivered to the Germans.
All the children.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
And I remember all the mothers with little children came to our house, and they were saying to my father, please save us, save us.
And my father said, there's nothing I can do.
And they said, Mr.
Rybowski, then can we stay in your house?
They felt that whatever will happen to-- they said, whatever will happen to your family will happen to us.
And my father said, but-- but you can't all stay here with the children.
There's nothing I can do about it.
And some friends of ours who had young children came from the other side of the ghetto.
It was like they thought that my father can perform miracles.
And I think that my sister went to see [?
Voik, ?] because she came home and she said to my dad that [?
Voik ?] said that there's nothing he'll be able to do, because he won't even be in the ghetto at this time, because this is a different department of Germans who's going to come in.
They were called the [GERMAN].
[GERMAN] is like somebody comes suddenly and falls on you.
You know?
So he sent word that my father should hide me and my brother.
So [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we had a basement in the floor, and my father fixed it up a little bit.
And he said, when the Germans come, the two of you will go into this basement.
How big was the basement?
Oh, it wasn't big.
It was about, I would say, five by five, the most.
No, it was in the ground.
We lived on the main floor.
It was in the ground.
And the Polish people always had a place like that to keep-- it was a storage place to keep food that shouldn't spoil, because it was always cold down there.
But my father fixed it up a little bit, that we could--
He put a couple stools there, that we could sit on the stools, and a jar of water.
And we used to have drills, and used to go in there.
You know?
And everything went fine until the day came.
And when the day came, I didn't want to go in.
I just refused to go in.
I was so scared, because I heard stories that if they found someone who was hiding, they shoot them immediately, that they put a gun to their head and shoot them.
And I started to cry, I don't want to be shot.
I don't want to be shot.
And I wouldn't go in.
Of course, my brother didn't either.
So when they came, and-- we had to leave the house.
We had to go outside.
Supposedly they were after children-- little children.
So we also had the neighbor that had a little boy, and at that time he must've been about two years old.

So my father fixed the attic for her to hide in there, and when the Germans came and they were yelling, raus, raus, and we all had to go outside and stand in-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] at attention, and they were going from one to the other, and--
I don't know how it happened, but at one point, I-- my dad took my arm and he said-- he pointed to a house in the back of me, and he said, run.
And I run.
And I run into the house, and I stayed in the house until the action was over.
We knew the people.
They were also people-- as a matter of fact, the man that lived there was a Jewish policeman.
There was nobody in the house when I came in, because they were all outside, too.
And by the time this was over, when I came out, when the Germans left, my mother was gone.
They took my mother.
[PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] Sorry.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I always felt it was my fault.
[SOBBING] I always felt that my father worried too much about me, and didn't worry about her.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] So.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I'll compose myself.
It was supposed to be, but it wasn't.
It wasn't.
Because they took my aunt and my cousins the same day.
They took whoever they wanted, and-- No.
[INAUDIBLE].
They took whoever they wanted.
They took a lot of people beside the children.
So That was-- thank you.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That was the first action I was in.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Then we just picked up our lives.
My mother was gone.
There's nothing we could have done.
My sister tried-- she tried to go to the Jewish police.
She tried to-- but no one could do nothing.
And my mother wore a sweater that my sister knitted for her.
And it was a very unusual, beautiful sweater.
And a few days later, we got the sweater back.
So we knew.
And then we just-- just kept on going.
Life just went on.
And there were other problems.
My brother got sick.
And we just did the best we could.
And we-- came the spring, the summer, we had some greenery.
We had something to eat.
Prior to that, we used to go out and pick whatever we thought was edible, including, we found out that poison ivy, the European kind of poison ivy, is edible once it's cooked.
So we used to go into the fields and pick that and eat.
There were other little things that grew that could have been eaten.
We just-- whatever there was.
We used to get a ration of so-called coffee.
It consisted of-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that was barley, burned barley.
And of course, it was burned black.
But once you boiled it, the water would turn black, that was our coffee.
And we ate the barley that was left.
Oh, it was terrible, because it had the husks on it.
But we used to-- we learned to make cake out of it.
How did we make cake?
We used the barley and a piece of bread to keep it together, and saccharin.
And we used to make a cake out of it.
Oh, we found so many things we could do with so many-- so that was one of the things we ate.
But whatever we could find.
It was-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we were always hungry.
Always.
There was never a day in the ghetto that I wasn't hungry.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
We found out that-- when we planted things, we couldn't wait till it grew, so we used to eat the leaves that came up, you know?
Before something develops in the ground, there are leaves, so we used to pick the leaves and eat them.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it was-- the rations we got were just almost nothing.
A piece of bread and a watery soup.
How can you survive on that?
And, you know, you-- either you suffer from malnutrition, or you just find other things to eat that you can get something from.
And that's what we did.
We just-- whatever was edible.
I think if-- if-- if there were any rats or mice in the ghetto, we would have eaten them.
But unfortunately, there weren't any, because there was no food.
We tried to catch birds, but after a while, there were no birds.
It never dawned on me till after the war, to-- one day when I heard the birds singing, I said, my god, there were no birds in the ghetto.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I remember why.
There was no food.
No animal would go where there was no food.
So we couldn't even dream of catching a bird.
And it just-- it was just a horrible time.
